---
output: github_document
---

<!-- README.md is generated from README.Rmd. Please edit that file -->

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

# aquacroptools <img src="man/figures/logo.png" align="right" height="139"/>

<!-- badges: start -->
<!-- badges: end -->

R tools and wrappers for running FAO AquaCrop in reproducible workflows.

## Overview

AquaCrop is a crop simulation model developed by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) to simulate crop yield response to water availability, climate variability, and management practices. Its strong scientific foundation and relatively low data requirements have made it a reference tool for research, operational studies, and climate impact assessments worldwide.

Running AquaCrop at scale, however, can be tedious. The model relies on a file-based workflow — each simulation requires manually preparing a set of formatted input files for climate, soil, crop, and management parameters, then invoking the executable, and finally parsing the output files one by one. This becomes a bottleneck as soon as simulations span multiple sites, years, or scenarios.

**aquacroptools** solves this by providing a lightweight R interface that automates the entire workflow. It handles input file generation, batch simulation orchestration, and output parsing — all from R, all scriptable, and all compatible with standard tidyverse and spatial workflows. The model engine itself is never touched: aquacroptools always delegates computation to the official FAO AquaCrop binary, ensuring full scientific validity and consistency with published results.

### What you can do with aquacroptools

- **Generate input files programmatically** — convert R data frames directly into AquaCrop-compatible climate, soil, crop, and management files, with no manual editing.
- **Run batch simulations at scale** — automate simulations across station networks, climate scenarios, planting date calendars, or multi-year periods.
- **Parse and tidy outputs** — read AquaCrop result files into structured data frames ready for analysis, visualization, or export.
- **Build reproducible workflows** — script the full chain from raw data to final results, enabling version control, traceability, and collaboration.

### Who it is for

aquacroptools is aimed at agronomists, climate scientists, hydrologists, and data analysts working on crop-water-climate interactions — whether for regional yield assessments, climate change impact studies, irrigation optimization, operational crop monitoring, or research and capacity building.

## Installation

```r
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("mwaongo/aquacroptools")
```

## Getting started

The first step is to set up a dedicated project directory. `init_aquacrop()` creates the standard AquaCrop folder structure and downloads the official FAO binary appropriate for your operating system.

```r
library(aquacroptools)

init_aquacrop(path = "my-project", version = "7.2")
```

This will open by default a new clean RStudio project. Consult readme.txt, in the root of the project for more informations.

### Climate

aquacroptools ships with a built-in `weather` dataset containing daily observations for a single station, which you can use to explore the workflow. A single call to `write_climate()` generates all four climate input files required by AquaCrop.

```r
data("weather")  # columns: year, month, day, rain, et0, tmin, tmax

write_climate(data = weather, path = "CLIMATE/", site_name = "wakanda")
# → CLIMATE/wakanda.PLU  (rainfall)
# → CLIMATE/wakanda.Tnx  (min/max temperature)
# → CLIMATE/wakanda.ETo  (reference evapotranspiration)
# → CLIMATE/wakanda.CLI  (master file linking the three above)
```

If you need finer control over file content or naming, individual writers (`write_plu()`, `write_tnx()`, `write_eto()`, `write_cli()`) are also available.

### Crop and soil

```r
# Crop parameter file — override individual parameters via params = list(...)
write_cro(path = "CROP/", crop_name = "maize")

# Soil profile + initial water content in one call
write_sol(
  site_name = "wakanda",
  path      = "SOIL/",
  texture   = c("loam", "clay loam"),  # one entry per layer
  thickness = c(0.3, 0.7)
)
# → SOIL/wakanda.SOL
# → SOIL/wakanda.SW0  (written automatically, use write_sw0 = FALSE to skip)
```

### Project file

`write_prm()` assembles all inputs into a single `.PRM` file, writing one simulation block per row of `planting_schedule`.

```r
write_prm(
  site_name         = "wakanda",
  path              = "LIST/",
  planting_schedule = data.frame(year = 2010:2020, planting_doy = 121),
  crop_name         = "maize",
  crop_path         = "CROP/",
  soil_path         = "SOIL/",
  climate_path      = "CLIMATE/",
  base_path         = getwd()
)
# → LIST/wakanda.PRM  (11 year blocks, one per row of planting_schedule)
```

When planting dates are not fixed but instead depend on seasonal rainfall onset, you can provide a `.CAL` file and set `calendar_path`. In this case `find_onset()` is called internally to derive the planting schedule — you do not need to supply it manually.

```r
write_prm(
  site_name     = "wakanda",
  path          = "LIST/",
  calendar_path = "CAL/",   # onset criterion defined in wakanda.CAL
  crop_name     = "maize",
  crop_path     = "CROP/",
  soil_path     = "SOIL/",
  climate_path  = "CLIMATE/",
  base_path     = getwd()
)
```

### Running the simulation

```r
run_aquacrop()
```

## Batch simulations

`write_prm_batch()` processes multiple stations in a single call. When all stations share the same planting schedule, pass a single data frame and it will be applied to every station.

```r
stations <- c("site_01", "site_02", "site_03")

write_prm_batch(
  site_name         = stations,
  crop_name         = "maize",
  planting_schedule = data.frame(year = 1981:2020, planting_doy = 201),
  crop_duration     = 90,
  base_path         = getwd()
)
```

When stations have different planting schedules, use `purrr::walk2()` directly on `write_prm()`, as `write_prm_batch()` does not accept a list of data frames.

```r
library(purrr)

schedules <- list(
  site_01 = data.frame(year = 1981:2020, planting_doy = 195),
  site_02 = data.frame(year = 1981:2020, planting_doy = 201),
  site_03 = data.frame(year = 1981:2020, planting_doy = 210)
)

walk2(stations, schedules, \(site, sched) {
  write_prm(
    site_name         = site,
    path              = "LIST/",
    planting_schedule = sched,
    crop_name         = "maize",
    crop_path         = "CROP/",
    soil_path         = "SOIL/",
    climate_path      = "CLIMATE/",
    base_path         = getwd()
  )
})
```

For calendar-based onset detection across many stations, `write_prm_batch()` calls `find_onset()` automatically for each station. Set `site_name = NULL` to let it discover all available stations from the climate directory.

```r
write_prm_batch(
  site_name     = NULL,       # discovers all *.CLI files in CLIMATE/
  crop_name     = "maize",
  calendar_path = "CAL/",
  base_path     = getwd()
)
```

## Reading input files

All climate and calendar input files can be read back into tidy data frames for inspection or further processing.

```r
read_climate("CLIMATE/wakanda.CLI")  # master file + data
read_plu("CLIMATE/wakanda.PLU")
read_tnx("CLIMATE/wakanda.Tnx")
read_eto("CLIMATE/wakanda.ETo")      # alias: read_et0()
read_cal("CAL/wakanda.CAL")          # onset parameters
```

## Key functions

| File type        | Writer             | Batch writer         |
|------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Climate (all)    | `write_climate()`  | —                    |
| Rainfall         | `write_plu()`      | —                    |
| Temperature      | `write_tnx()`      | —                    |
| Reference ET     | `write_eto()`      | —                    |
| Crop             | `write_cro()`      | —                    |
| Soil profile     | `write_sol()`      | `write_sol_batch()`  |
| Management       | `write_man()`      | `write_man_batch()`  |
| Irrigation       | `write_irr()`      | `write_irr_batch()`  |
| Calendar (onset) | `write_cal()`      | `write_cal_batch()`  |
| Project file     | `write_prm()`      | `write_prm_batch()`  |
